title: Check Sudo Permissions
description: Lists the commands that the current user is permitted to run with elevated privileges (usually as root) via the sudo command.
when_to_use: This should be the very first command you type upon gaining a low-privilege shell on a Linux box. If the user can run commands like 'vim', 'awk', or 'nmap' via sudo without a password, you can often break out into a root shell immediately.
common_pitfalls: Often prompts for the user's password. If you don't have the user's password, this check might fail unless the administrator explicitly configured NOPASSWD.

title: Search for SUID Binaries
description: Searches the entire file system for executable files that have the Set-User-ID (SUID) bit set. When executed, these files run with the privileges of the file's owner (often root) rather than the user executing them.
when_to_use: A fundamental manual check. If you find a non-standard binary (e.g., an outdated version of 'exim4' or a custom backup script) with the SUID bit set, it is a prime target for exploitation.
common_pitfalls: This will return many false positives (like /usr/bin/passwd or /bin/su) which naturally require the SUID bit to function properly. You must filter these out mentally.

title: View System Cron Jobs
description: Reads the system-wide crontab file and lists the contents of the cron.d directory to reveal scheduled tasks running automatically.
when_to_use: Look for scheduled tasks running as root that call scripts with weak permissions. If you can edit a script that root is scheduled to run every 5 minutes, you can simply append a reverse shell payload to it.

title: Search for File Capabilities
description: Recursively searches the filesystem for binaries that have been assigned specific Linux capabilities (a modern alternative to the monolithic SUID bit).
when_to_use: Crucial for finding stealthy privilege escalation vectors. For example, if the 'tar' or 'python' binary has the 'cap_dac_read_search' capability, you can use it to read any file on the system, including /etc/shadow, without needing full root.
common_pitfalls: Often overlooked by beginners who only focus on SUID files. Capabilities are widely used in containerized environments (like Docker).

title: Run Full LinPEAS Scan
description: Downloads and executes the Linux Privilege Escalation Awesome Script. It color-codes the output to highlight the most probable paths to root.
when_to_use: The gold standard for automated enumeration. Run this to check for vulnerable kernel versions, misconfigured NFS shares, plaintext passwords in config files, and much more.
common_pitfalls: Executing scripts directly from the internet via 'curl | sh' is extremely loud and easily blocked or flagged by enterprise EDR solutions.
